annotations.label: Distributive property (informal)

anchor.snippet: the distributive property of binary operations is a generalization of the distributive law

annotations.note: The `Distrib` typeclass bundles the left- and right-distributive laws for a multiplication over an addition, matching the informal definition.

annotations.label: Arithmetic example of distributivity

anchor.snippet: For example, in elementary arithmetic

annotations.note: Concrete arithmetic identities like 2┬Ę(1+3)=2┬Ę1+2┬Ę3 are instances of `left_distrib` (a ┬Ę (b + c) = a ┬Ę b + a ┬Ę c) applied on `ŌäĢ`/`Ōäż`.

annotations.label: Left-distributive operation

anchor.snippet: the operation [MATH] is left-distributive over (or with respect to) [MATH] if, given any elements

annotations.note: `LeftDistribClass` axiomatizes `a * (b + c) = a * b + a * c`, exactly the left-distributivity condition.

annotations.label: Right-distributive operation

anchor.snippet: the operation [MATH] is right-distributive over [MATH] if, given any elements

annotations.note: `RightDistribClass` axiomatizes `(a + b) * c = a * c + b * c`, exactly the right-distributivity condition.

annotations.label: Distributive operation

anchor.snippet: and the operation [MATH] is distributive over [MATH] if it is left- and right-distributive

annotations.note: `Distrib` bundles both `left_distrib` and `right_distrib`, matching the two-sided distributivity definition.

annotations.label: Equivalence under commutativity

anchor.snippet: When [MATH] is commutative , the three conditions above are logically equivalent

annotations.note: No named Mathlib lemma packages the three-way equivalence of left-/right-/two-sided distributivity assuming commutativity of the outer operation.

annotations.label: Left vs right distributivity under commutativity

anchor.snippet: If the operation outside the parentheses (in this case, the multiplication) is commutative, then left-distributivity implies right-distributivity

annotations.note: Mathlib has no standalone lemma converting `LeftDistribClass` into `RightDistribClass` via commutativity of the outer operation.

annotations.label: Division is only right-distributive

anchor.snippet: One example of an operation that is "only" right-distributive is division

annotations.note: `add_div` states `(a + b) / c = a / c + b / c`, capturing the right-distributivity of division over addition.

annotations.label: Distributive laws in rings and fields

anchor.snippet: The distributive laws are among the axioms for rings

annotations.note: `Ring` extends `Distrib` so the distributive axioms are built into the ring class.

annotations.label: Multiplying sums (expansion rule)

anchor.snippet: When a sum is multiplied by a sum, multiply each summand of a sum with each summand of the other sum

annotations.note: `Finset.sum_mul_sum` expresses a product of two finite sums as the double sum of pairwise products.

annotations.label: Mental arithmetic via distributivity

anchor.snippet: During mental arithmetic, distributivity is often used unconsciously

annotations.note: The mental-arithmetic trick is a routine use of `left_distrib`/`right_distrib` on concrete numbers.

annotations.label: Factoring via distributivity

anchor.snippet: Since the factor [MATH] occurs in all summands, it can be factored out

annotations.note: Factoring is `mul_add`/`left_distrib` applied right-to-left.

annotations.label: Distributivity of matrix multiplication

anchor.snippet: The distributive law is valid for matrix multiplication

annotations.note: `Matrix.mul_add` and `Matrix.add_mul` give the two-sided distributivity of matrix multiplication over matrix addition.

annotations.label: Ordinal multiplication is only left-distributive

anchor.snippet: Multiplication of ordinal numbers , in contrast, is only left-distributive, not right-distributive

annotations.note: `Ordinal.leftDistribClass` instances `LeftDistribClass Ordinal`; no `RightDistribClass` instance is provided, matching the asymmetric situation.

annotations.label: Cross product distributes over vector addition

anchor.snippet: The cross product is left- and right-distributive over vector addition

annotations.note: `crossProduct` is defined as a bilinear `LinearMap`, so left/right additivity (and hence distributivity over vector addition) is built in via the `LinearMap` structure.

annotations.label: Union and intersection mutually distribute

anchor.snippet: For sets , the union is distributive over intersection , and intersection is distributive over union

annotations.note: `Set.inter_union_distrib_left` and `Set.union_inter_distrib_left` give the two mutual distributivity laws for set operations.

annotations.label: Disjunction and conjunction mutually distribute

anchor.snippet: Logical disjunction ("or") is distributive over logical conjunction ("and"), and vice versa

annotations.note: `or_and_left` and `and_or_left` formalize the mutual distributivity of `Ōł©` and `Ōł¦` on `Prop`.

annotations.label: Max and min mutually distribute

anchor.snippet: the maximum operation is distributive over the minimum operation, and vice versa

annotations.note: `max_min_distrib_left` and `min_max_distrib_left` give the mutual distributivity of `max` and `min` on linear orders.

annotations.label: gcd and lcm mutually distribute

anchor.snippet: the greatest common divisor is distributive over the least common multiple

annotations.note: Searches in `Mathlib.Algebra.GCDMonoid` and `Nat`/`Int` gcd files turn up no `gcd_lcm`/`lcm_gcd` distributivity lemma.

annotations.label: Addition distributes over max and min

anchor.snippet: For real numbers, addition distributes over the maximum operation, and also over the minimum operation

annotations.note: `max_add_add_left` (resp. `min_add_add_left`) states `a + max b c = max (a + b) (a + c)`, capturing the distributivity of `+` over `max`/`min`.

annotations.label: FOIL method for binomial multiplication

anchor.snippet: For binomial multiplication, distribution is sometimes referred to as the FOIL Method

annotations.note: FOIL `(a+b)(c+d) = ac+ad+bc+bd` is an immediate consequence of `mul_add`/`add_mul`; not bundled as a separate named lemma but routinely closed by `ring`.

annotations.label: Distributivity in semirings

anchor.snippet: In all semirings , including the complex numbers

annotations.note: `Semiring` extends `Distrib`, so distributivity is part of the class definition.

annotations.label: Distributivity in algebras over a field

anchor.snippet: In all algebras over a field , including the octonions and other non-associative algebras

annotations.note: `Algebra` builds on `Semiring`/`Ring`, so distributivity is inherited; non-associative variants such as `NonUnitalNonAssocSemiring` are also present.

annotations.label: Distribution rule of replacement

anchor.snippet: distribution [ 3 ] [ 4 ] in logical proofs uses two valid rules of replacement

annotations.note: The two `Ōł©`/`Ōł¦` distribution equivalences used as rules of replacement are `or_and_left` and `and_or_left`.

annotations.label: Distributivity of truth-functional connectives

anchor.snippet: The following logical equivalences demonstrate that distributivity is a property of particular connectives

annotations.note: Mathlib/`Init` provide `and_or_left`, `or_and_left`, etc., capturing the standard distributivity equivalences for propositional connectives.

annotations.label: Distributivity failure in floating-point arithmetic

anchor.snippet: the distributive property of multiplication (and division) over addition may fail because of the limitations of arithmetic precision

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma states or quantifies the failure of distributivity for IEEE `Float`/`FloatArray` arithmetic.

annotations.label: Semiring

anchor.snippet: A semiring has two binary operations

annotations.note: `Semiring` is the standard Mathlib class for a semiring with the two distributivity axioms.

annotations.label: Ring as semiring with additive inverses

anchor.snippet: A ring is a semiring with additive inverses

annotations.note: `Ring` extends `Semiring` together with an `AddGroup` structure, exactly matching the informal definition.

annotations.label: Distributive lattice

anchor.snippet: A lattice is another kind of algebraic structure with two binary operations

annotations.note: `DistribLattice` is the Mathlib class for lattices satisfying the distributivity of `ŌŖö` over `ŌŖō` (equivalently the dual).

annotations.label: Boolean algebra as ring or distributive lattice

anchor.snippet: A Boolean algebra can be interpreted either as a special kind of ring

annotations.note: `BooleanAlgebra` (in `Mathlib.Order.BooleanAlgebra.Defs`) and `BooleanRing` are both formalized, and `BooleanRing.toBooleanAlgebra` connects them.

annotations.label: Product of sums equals sum of products

anchor.snippet: In any semiring, distributivity can be used to show that any product of sums is a sum of products

annotations.note: `Finset.sum_mul_sum` rewrites a product of two `Finset` sums as the double sum of pairwise products.

annotations.label: Near-rings and near-fields

anchor.snippet: Structures without two-sided distributive laws are near-rings and near-fields

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `NearRing` or `NearField` typeclass; `decl_exists` for these names returns false and grep finds no near-ring structure.

annotations.label: Sub- and super-distributivity

anchor.snippet: the notions of sub-distributivity , where equality is replaced by "less than or equal"

annotations.note: No `Subdistrib`/`Superdistrib` classes appear in Mathlib; only specific inequality lemmas (e.g. `Nat.mul_add_le`) exist sporadically.

annotations.label: Distributive law of monads

anchor.snippet: if [MATH] and [MATH] are monads on a category

annotations.note: Loogle finds no `distrib`-named declaration mentioning `Monad` in the category-theoretic sense; the Beck distributive law between two monads is not formalized.

annotations.label: Antidistributive property of inversion

anchor.snippet: The ubiquitous identity that relates inverses to the binary operation in any group

annotations.note: `mul_inv_rev` states `(a * b)Ōü╗┬╣ = bŌü╗┬╣ * aŌü╗┬╣` in any `DivisionMonoid`, capturing the antidistributivity of inversion over the group operation.

annotations.label: Antidistributive elements in a near-ring

anchor.snippet: one can speak of (two-sided) distributive elements but also of antidistributive elements

annotations.note: Since near-rings themselves are absent from Mathlib, the notion of antidistributive elements in a near-ring is also not formalized.

annotations.label: Antidistributive laws for implication

anchor.snippet: the term antidistributive law is sometimes used to denote the interchange between conjunction and disjunction when implication factors over them

annotations.note: `imp_and` (`a ŌåÆ (b Ōł¦ c) Ōåö (a ŌåÆ b) Ōł¦ (a ŌåÆ c)`) and `or_imp` (`(a Ōł© b) ŌåÆ c Ōåö (a ŌåÆ c) Ōł¦ (b ŌåÆ c)`) formalize the antidistributive laws for `ŌåÆ` over `Ōł¦`/`Ōł©`.
